role_hints.role: Production and lettershop operations (print, insert, sort, mail)
role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10 — the record's role_mix is 4-digit 5418 (ad-agency-dominated) and hides the printing-press operators (51-5112), mail machine operators (43-9051) and bindery/insertion crews that dominate actual direct-mail houses; long tail, est. ~30-40% of a real 541860 wage bill
role_hints.note: Physical print, inserting, sorting and postal logistics are machine-operated but human-run; robotics not current-AI automates them. The record's low labor share (0.2807) reflects heavy materials/postage pass-through, consistent with a production business.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists 6.6% of wage bill (5418 proxy); plus data-processing staff in the unlisted tail, est. ~5-8%
role_hints.note: List hygiene, merge/purge, NCOA processing, segmentation and response modeling are near-fully automatable — this is the industry's white-collar core and the most AI-exposed line.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents 8.2% + 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.0% (5418 proxy)
role_hints.note: Quoting, job specs and proofs automate; client relationships with agencies, nonprofits and financial-services mailers persist and are often personal to owners.

role_hints.oews_basis: 27-1024 Graphic Designers 3.3% + 27-1011 Art Directors 4.1% (5418 proxy, agency-weighted — thinner in real mail houses)
role_hints.note: Direct-mail creative, copy and versioning are directly AI-generable; many houses already template it.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 9.3% + 11-2021 Marketing Managers 5.2% (5418 proxy)
role_hints.note: Plant scheduling, postal-rate optimization and job costing automate substantially; customer escalation and vendor negotiation persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: long tail of office/admin occupations not in the listed top-10, est. remainder ~15-20%
role_hints.note: Order entry, billing, postal paperwork (statements of mailing) and CSR work automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: PRINTING United Alliance State of the Industry reports
source_hints.why: The print trade association (PIA/SGIA merged); tracks technology adoption and financial benchmarks across commercial print including mail production.

source_hints.source: USPS RPW (Revenue, Pieces, Weight) quarterly reports
source_hints.why: Primary data on Marketing Mail volume and postage trends — postage is the dominant cost line and the demand-melt indicator for the whole medium.
The standard forecast for US direct-mail ad spend — evidence on whether the medium's revenue base is growing or shrinking.

source_hints.source: ANA (Association of National Advertisers) response-rate / direct-mail research (successor to DMA data)
source_hints.why: DMA folded into ANA in 2019; its response-rate and channel-performance series is the classic direct-mail effectiveness benchmark — confirm what ANA still publishes.

source_hints.source: New Direction Partners printing M&A commentary (via Printing Impressions)
source_hints.why: The most-cited advisory practice for small commercial-print/mail M&A; publishes rule-of-thumb valuation multiples for print businesses.

source_hints.source: PRINTING United Alliance / NAPCO owner-demographics and succession surveys
source_hints.why: Commercial print and mail is a known aging-owner industry; association surveys carry succession-intent data.

capture_questions: Direct-mail jobs are bid per-piece with postage passed through at cost — when AI collapses the data-prep, creative and admin cost lines, does competitive bidding among mail houses pass the savings straight to clients as lower per-piece prices (the deflation channel), or do switching costs and postal-logistics expertise let incumbents hold price?
Who owns the client relationship — the mail house, or the agencies and brokers who route jobs to whichever shop bids lowest? What share of a typical shop's volume is agency-intermediated vs. direct?

Can clients bypass the shop's white-collar layer with generic AI (list hygiene, creative, versioning) and buy only commodity print-and-mail execution — collapsing the value-added margin while leaving the low-margin physical work?

Postage is 50%+ of a campaign's cost and rises faster than inflation — does the operator have any capture lever at all on the dominant cost line, or is its margin confined to the shrinking production/data slice?
terminal_value_question: AI does not print or insert mail — the physical product survives automation — so the terminal question is not AI replacing the operator but whether paid demand for the medium survives at all: Marketing Mail volumes are in secular decline and USPS rate hikes compound it, while AI simultaneously makes the digital substitutes cheaper and better-targeted. Is this operator's AI story a cost cut on a melting revenue base — and does the melt rate (check USPS RPW volume trend) exceed any plausible margin gain?
special_notes: Role-mix contamination: the record's role_mix is 4-digit 5418 (ad agencies dominate). Actual direct-mail houses are production plants — press operators, inserters, mail-machine operators — none of which appear in the listed top-10. Re-weight the V breakdown toward physical production before setting within-role fractions; the 0.2807 labor share (materials/postage-heavy) corroborates this.

Pricing-model deflation candidate: per-piece competitive bidding with postage pass-through. AI savings in data/creative/admin are the exact lines clients can re-bid; model pi_dist with the bid-competition channel explicit.

Secular demand decline: single-year revenue snapshots overstate stability — USPS Marketing Mail volume has fallen for over a decade and rate increases (multiple per year since 2021) accelerate advertiser exit. Cyclicality plus melt distorts multiples; small mail houses trade at low, asset-flavored multiples (often quoted on % of revenue or with equipment value), not clean EBITDA multiples.

EBITDA normalization: family-owned shops carry owner comp inside SDE and often own the plant real estate — separate real-estate rent and normalize owner salaries before comparing to any platform multiple.

Boundary confusion: commercial printing is 323111 (many 'direct mail printers' classify there), advertising material distribution (door-to-door, shared mail like Valpak) is 541870, mailing-list publishing is 511140-adjacent, and fulfillment/3PL houses sit in 561499 — association benchmarks (PRINTING United) span commercial print broadly; check segment coverage before quoting.

The 500+ class (28 firms, avg $122M revenue) includes national players (e.g., large mail/marketing-services groups); the roll-up-relevant middle is the 20-99 class (199 firms). n_band 261 uses a generic 15.65% margin — print/mail margins are typically thinner (5-10% EBITDA), so n_band is likely overstated; flag when scoring N.
